name: Brute-Force Login
title: Brute-force logon activity against {user} from {attacker_ip}
description: Over 20 failed logons (EventCode 4625) against account {user} on {host} from external IP {attacker_ip} within 4 minutes, followed by a successful logon (4624).
objective: Determine whether the brute-force attempt against {user} succeeded, establish the scope of the attacker's access, and decide whether this requires escalation.
checklist: Run the detection query and confirm the volume and timing of failed logons (4625)
Identify the source IP and check whether it is internal or external
Search for a successful logon (4624) for the same account from the same source IP
Check what the account did after the successful logon (pivot: user={user})
Verify whether other accounts were targeted from the same source IP
Document findings and recommend containment (disable account / block IP)
hints: Failed logons are EventCode 4625; success is 4624. Try: index=windows EventCode=4625 user={user}
The key question is whether a 4624 from {attacker_ip} appears AFTER the burst of 4625s. Sort by time.
A success following a password-guessing burst means compromised credentials — this is a true positive that needs escalation for credential reset and IP blocking.
expectedEvidence: Burst of 4625 failed logons for {user} from {attacker_ip}
Successful 4624 logon from the same external IP after the failures
Confirmation the source IP is external / not a known VPN egress
explanation: This was a successful brute-force attack. The pattern to recognize: a high-frequency burst of 4625 failures for a single account from a single external IP, terminated by a 4624 success — the guess landed. Failed-only bursts are common internet noise, but a success changes everything: credentials are compromised. Correct response is escalation: disable/reset the account, block the source IP, and hunt for post-compromise activity. Key discriminator vs. false positive: locked-out users generate 4625s too, but from internal IPs, at human speed, without a foreign success.
reportTemplate: 1. Disable and reset credentials for account {user}. 2. Block {attacker_ip} at the perimeter. 3. Review all activity by {user} after the successful logon. 4. Check for MFA gaps on externally exposed authentication.

events.message: An account failed to log on. Account Name: {user} Failure Reason: Unknown user name or bad password. Logon Type: 3 Source Network Address: {attacker_ip}
An account was successfully logged on. Account Name: {user} Logon Type: 3 Source Network Address: {attacker_ip}
A new process has been created. New Process Name: C:\Windows\System32\whoami.exe Creator Process: cmd.exe Account: {user}
